insights: Higher-cost expenses are processed 4 times faster than lower-cost ones, with Q1_Low expenses averaging 32.1 days versus Q4_VeryHigh at 7.7 days — a consistent, monotonic inverse relationship across all cost brackets.

This inverse cost-processing time relationship holds universally across all 7 departments, 4 categories, and 53 users, confirming it is a system-wide prioritization pattern rather than an artifact of departmental workflows, category-specific policies, or user-level specialization.

State distribution (Pending, Submitted, Approved, Declined) is nearly identical across cost brackets — with no meaningful difference in workflow stage composition — ruling out differential approval paths or expedited state transitions as the explanation for faster high-cost processing.

User assignment is highly specialized by cost bracket: 17 users handle ≥60% high-cost expenses while another 17 handle ≤33%, yet the same users still process their own Q4_VeryHigh expenses ~4x faster than their own Q1_Low expenses, proving the speed differential is embedded in the processing logic, not user capability or workload selection.

The 31.6% unprocessed expenses (missing processed_date) are concentrated in Pending/Submitted states and show no cost-bracket skew — meaning the observed 4x speed advantage applies only to completed cases and does not reflect incomplete or stalled high-cost reports.

Travel is the only category with full representation across all four cost brackets, and it exhibits the strongest version of the inverse pattern (Q1_Low: 34.2 days → Q4_VeryHigh: 6.8 days), suggesting this category may serve as the most reliable benchmark for policy calibration.
summary: The analysis conclusively demonstrates that expense processing time is strongly and inversely tied to cost — higher-cost expenses are processed approximately four times faster than lower-cost ones — and this pattern is systemic, not attributable to departmental, categorical, workflow-stage, or user-level variation. The consistency across all organizational dimensions indicates an embedded prioritization mechanism, likely reflecting implicit or explicit policy rules that accelerate high-value submissions. This raises material fairness concerns, as low-cost expenses — often routine, recurring, or employee-initiated — bear disproportionate delays, potentially undermining trust, compliance, and operational equity. Given the robustness of the finding and its cross-dimensional stability, the immediate next focus should be auditing existing expense policy documentation and approval routing logic to identify whether cost-based prioritization is intentional, justified, and transparently governed — and if not, to redesign triage criteria to balance efficiency with procedural fairness.
